# Graphic Design History A Critical Guide

![](images/9df4418b033e8f264c7baee62207043b7dfa6503e19a91f4ad19f34b44461209.jpg)

Johanna Drucker Emily McVarish

![](images/22037f82aaf2d403a287aa36636999850abaf2a2fe560c2d2d22fad4e516762e.jpg)

Johanna Drucker is the Robertson Professor of Media Studies and English at the University of Virginia. She has been on the faculty at Yale University, SUNY Purchase, Columbia University, and the University of Texas at Dallas, as well as holding a Mellon Faculty Fellowship at Harvard, a Fulbright Fellowship, and a Getty Fellowship. Her previous publications include The Alphabetic Labyrinth (1995), The Century of Artists' Books (1995), The Visible Word (1994), and Sweet Dreams (2005). She is also known for her work as a book artist and experimental visual poet.

![](images/278b77efcd6ab54508f798bbe26ca6c430fa2070e854533d61b6b0604aa0ab0a.jpg)

Emily McVarish is Assistant Professor of Graphic Design at California College of the Arts where she teaches experimental typography, media theory, and topical studios. A writer, designer, and book artist, she has maintained a hybrid practice in San Francisco since 1990. Her work has been exhibited internationally, published by Granary Books, and collected by Harvard University and the British Library among other major libraries and museums. Her writing has been featured in Visible Language.

Preface This book evolved from our practice as artists and teachers. We both make use of theory and research in our creative work, and we are regularly struck by the realization that many of the models we follow and forms we use have long histories. Insight into the role of history in daily practice can be thrilling, just as critical concepts can be powerful tools for thinking about a design process or product. Our sheer enthusiasm for these ideas and a desire to share them led to this project. We didn't want to tell the story of graphic design, as if it were something that happened in the past, but to expose the many principles of current composition and communication that are grounded in historical precedent.

Our complementary perspectives brought this project to life. We were both trained in the practical aspects of layout and printing, typography and visual expression, book arts and publication design, but we were also deeply influenced by theoretical texts we read in the 1980s when structuralism and post-structuralism were in the air. From these readings we gleaned a language to describe the life of images and texts. Elements of cultural studies and art history, theories of representation and signification, threads of intellectual history and visual studies have proved stimulating and useful to us in keeping our own creative processes in play. This book is a synthesis of these many strains of thought. It presents ideas that are basic to studio and scholarly practice in the field of graphic design.

Our work with students prompted and framed many of the questions that we address here. Johanna Drucker was teaching art history at Yale when she first began to work with the ideas that animate this book. The student body combined sophisticated graphic designers and open-minded art historians—a terrific constituency through which to explore the intersection of art and design. In the early 1990s, Johanna taught a course on the dialogue between modern art and graphics, with an emphasis on the exciting period from the turn of the nineteenth century through the middle of the twentieth century. That opportunity brought into focus the challenge of thinking about how borrowings from art history and critical theory might shape a history that would be appropriate and specific to graphic design. The solution was an approach based on analysis of social forces and conditions but grounded in the study of individual artifacts and works.

Emily McVarish teaches courses at California College of the Arts that integrate theoretical points of departure with studio process and critique. At CCA, as at other contemporary arts institutions, intellectual and practical disciplines are intertwined. A design problem is a thought experiment, not just a task on which formal or technical skills are brought to bear. Emily's teaching involves devising seminars and studios in which critical questions drive graphic design, and historical research becomes a direct instrument for realizing projects. In these courses, theoretical readings and hands-on exercises are combined to position students to develop their own informed approaches to design practice.

We set out to write a short book, a brief set of principles through which to engage with the history of graphic design. Several years and much work later, we present a book that retains the spirit of light and spaciousness with which we began. Our book is available to browsing and visual encounter but also lends itself to more systematic study. In its design, images and captions form their own units and are not subsumed by the body text. Our hope is that the book stimulates its reader in much the same way that a lively slide show or well curated exhibition might.

A desire frequently expressed by graphic design educators for a book that would begin with prehistory and trace the impulses of mark-making and early symbolic expression forward to present currents of thought inspired the span and shape of our book. Early chapters have enormous temporal sweep. Later chapters are focused on shorter periods and changes in the profession. The culture of graphic design has changed as much as the technology through which it realizes its tasks, but basic principles from the earliest circumstances of human communication and exchange are still operative. Our approach has attempted to keep historical perspectives in view while demonstrating profound continuities in the practice of graphic arts, visual expression, and design.

In this endeavor, we have drawn on many sources. All who work in this field are indebted to Philip Meggs, whose groundbreaking A History of Graphic Design is a major reference work that has provided a descriptive historical foundation for the field. We were inspired by insights into the cultural contexts of design practice contained in the admirably succinct Graphic Design: A Concise History by Richard Hollis. Our methodological approach was sympathetically informed by Paul Jobling and David Crowley's Graphic Design: Reproduction and Representation since 1800. But a greater influence on our critical approach came from the social historian Max Gallo's The Poster in History. That book's demonstration of the links between graphic works and the social forces and conditions of their production sparked epiphanies. Other classic works and writings by our peers, too numerous to mention here, are listed in the bibliography. These texts are of real value and use to a field that has only begun to formulate itself: the critical history of graphic design.

What drove this book more than anything was our genuine enthusiasm for the material. At every stage of research and writing, review and exchange, layout and production, we found ourselves deeply involved with the images in front of us. Working on early writing systems, Medieval letterforms, or the uses of information graphics, we were always discovering materials so compelling that we would say, "This stuff is the most interesting of all." If we manage to communicate some of that enthusiasm to our readers, for any part of the material we've covered, we will have reached our goal: to pass on an interest in looking at these images, now and in the future, as sources of inspiration for investigative study, creative production, and reflection on the ways our work participates in the broader culture and the history of its formative ideas.

Graphic design is never just there.

Graphic artifacts always serve a purpose and contain an agenda,

no matter how neutral or natural they appear to be.

Someone is addressing someone else, for some reason,

through every object of designed communication.

The graphic forms of design

are expressions of the forces that shape our lives.

We need a critical history of graphic design:

To provide ways of thinking about graphic form Interest in the historical inventory of graphic design is high. Practitioners plunder the vast archive of visual forms for inspiration. Students look for precedents. The industries of mass multimedia consume ideas at a terrifying pace. Rapidly expanding online and in-print graphic resources are accessible and ubiquitous. Designers seek striking models to make their work stand outin the felds of advertising, packagingidentity, publication, and information design.

But the history of ideas about form is difficult to recover. We need a vocabulary to grasp the way graphic design communicates. Beyond the catalogue of style samples, historical insight offers an understanding of how design takes shape within cultural systems. How are we to understand the relations between style and technology? Between cultural moments and forms of expression? Between the persuasive tactics of graphic design and the messages they embody?

When these questions are correlated with the history of graphic developments, critical dimensions come into stark relief. Ideas about form offer their own possibilities for increasing our understanding of the way graphic design works to reflect or create values in our time.

To gain insight into design as a cultural practice As a cultural phenomenon, graphic design is embedded in institutions. Design studios, advertising agencies, publishing houses, print shops, and media companies, as well as the schools and programs that provide professional training, are all shaped by cultural and historical forces.

As a profession, graphic design operates within a network of constraints. Legal and economic issues, business and trade conventions, social and cultural mores, and attitudes toward imagery, language, composition, and representation are invisible—but potent—influences on the individual designer and the feld as a whole. Graphic designers were once artisans of the manuscript page and painted sign. Then they were tradesmen in print shops, working at type cases and imposing tables. Then they became professionals with specialized skills. More recently, they have come to be thought of as artists—or even celebrities. As workers in the industries of consumer culture, how much choice do they have about the interests their work serves?

A critical approach to graphic design and its history is useful to anyone engaged in reading the cultural landscape of contemporary life. For designers, such an approach provides conceptual skills (ways of thinking) that extend their production capabilities (ways of making). Recognizing the historical dimension of the field sheds light on the choices that individual practitioners make on a daily basis—as well as the cultural dynamics within which they operate.

The shape of a critical history of graphic design Rather than tell the "story" of graphic design, we will outline some basic principles for thinking about the way historical origins inform contemporary practice. At issue is not just a history of designs but a history of ideas and assumptions about forms of communication.

Why does this page look the way it does? Why does it have margins and page numbers? Why are the lines this length? But even more, what assumptions about reading, about texts and information, and about imagined readers or actual audiences are encoded in its format? These questions can be answered, in part, by a purely formal analysis (with attention to proportion, balance, choice of typeface, size, and style). Skills for reading these visual properties are crucial tools.

But what historical aspects of graphic forms are invisible? A critical approach to graphic design exposes the rhetorical workings expressed in formal structures. (The shape of text blocks and use of page numbers express expectations about how this book will be read. These forms are the legacy of shifts in reading practices in the late Middle Ages, not just pleasing arrangements grounded in "universal" ideals of legibility.)

Graphic forms carry their history encoded as conventions, charged with cultural values—and loaded with social implications. The authority of any particular text is often linked to the codes of its presentation. (Does it look like a bible, an official document, or a tabloid newspaper?) In choosing a particular form of communication—whether it is a grid, type style, decorative element, or illustration—we perpetuate ways of thinking. Any system of formal organization expresses an attitude toward knowledge and its representation. Critically informed historical knowledge shows us how the structure of information becomes part of its meaning as form.

The critical approach to graphic design history is not a series of descriptions of artifacts but a set of theoretical frameworks for seeing the forces and conditions within which those objects came into being. The history of style is a history of culture. Analysis of form is a way to see that history reworked in the objects of contemporary life.

What, then, should we look at—and just as important, how?

## The definition of the field

Identity of the graphic designer

Activities, trades, skills, and capabilities that mark the designer's changing identity

## Professional institutions

Guilds, shops, studios, agencies,

schools, professional associations,

industry and government support

programs

## Internal discourse

Venues and means by which

the field acquires legitimacy:

trade publications, competitions

that foster critical attention and

recognition

Graphic design profession Graphic designers are defined by the tasks they perform, and our idea of a designer's identity at any given historical moment is a product of circumstances, not independent of those circumstances. The once anonymous practitioner may now aspire to "star" status. But graphic designers' work frequently ends up circulating without attribution, unmarked, as a free ion in the communications infosphere. Many graphic objects are made by committee or through a complicated chain of decisions in dialogue with a client. Design choices may be unwittingly constrained by cultural conventions about what is and is not allowed to be said or shown. Thus, a graphic artifact is as much a product of a complicated cultural system as it is the work of an individual designer. The designer's vision is often overwhelmed by professional exigencies. Meanwhile, the identity of the field as a whole changes in any given era. Critical reflection on graphic design developed mainly in the twentieth century, although harbingers of such internal discourse existed in the work of earlier practitioners—many of whom would not have known the term graphic designer or identified with the profession. Professional organizations have altered the landscape of graphic design, codifying a hierarchy of status and prestige within the field. But even as accomplished designers are now recognized with awards and prizes for their work, the majority of the public remain surprisingly ignorant of the names and contributions of those whose designs they make use of every day. Try subtracting everything set in typefaces designed by John Baskerville or Matthew Carter or Zuzana Licko from the visual landscape. The image of that gap-toothed universe makes a dramatic point.

## The making of an artifact

General technology

Inventions and innovations that

are widespread in their effects

but without which specific design

technologies could not emerge

Design production and reproduction processes and technology

Methods making and reproducig

a design through all stages of its

creation and circulation

Technology Style choices in design are not entirely under the control of designers but are related to the ways technology develops in a broader cultural sphere. Mass production required power sources and high-speed machines, which, in turn, were developed through industrial processes. But technology does not determine social or formal change; it operates within changing cultural circumstances. (Electrification did not create digital media; it simply contributed a necessary element to its development.) Possibilities for production are called into being by particular cultural needs or eclipsed by shifting desires. The capabilities of production always constitute affordances, within which design practices can be conceived as well as realized. Many technologies of graphic design have long aesthetic traditions associated with them. The development of new tools and materials often leaps ahead of the conception of a graphic style suited to them. The history of design is as filled with peculiar attempts to reconcile inherited formal models and new technologies as it is with the spontaneous invention of appropriate styles. Images look radically different when the main method of production shifts from pen and ink to metal, or from hand-drawn lithographic pictures to photographs. But the aesthetic of a particular medium develops in relation to ideas, not just as an automatic or immediate effect of a new material or tool. (Visual approaches, like the photographic close-up or montage, embody ideas about images as materials that can be manipulated and recombined rather than simply received as realistic documents.)

# Elements of form and format, from individual mark to overall convention

Text design Lettering, micro- and macrotypography, format

Imagery and Iconography Pictorial elements, their history and meaning

Decorative elements Motifs. ornaments. borders

Marks of production Visual sntax at the elmentary level of dots. lines, tone

Graphic logic and rhetoric Composition as organization and argument

Aesthetic sensibility Cultural history of attitudes toward form

Style Style can be studied at macro and micro levels. The method by which marks are made produces a "syntax"—formal graphic language—of images, letterforms, and decorative elements. Likewise, every material aspect (such as the slickness or softness of paper) participates in the graphic production of meaning. The conventions for picturing the world, or forms of address in a text, can appear perfectly natural, a simple statement of the way things are. But these appearances conceal the exclusionary tactics of their rhetoric. Many familiar conventions contain biases that carry cultural implications (for example, the north-south orientation of maps, the rationalization of space by perspective, or the representation of a person of a particular gender or class). Decorative elements and ornaments produce cultural identity in ways that are often rendered invisible by their use as mere motifs. (Where do we get our ideas of what looks "Japanese" in a style like "Japonisme"?) Even our basic understanding of letterforms changes. Renaissance type designer Geoffrey Tory saw letters as symbols of cosmological principles, whereas the twentieth-century designer Ed Fella sees in them vivid expressions of vernacular poetics. The very shapes and proportions of alphabetical forms are products of cultural influences. Such notions change constantly. Every graphic artifact expresses a point of view, implicitly or explicitly, that participates in power structures, asserting subtle ano

Social relations, practices, and processes that have a major impact on graphic design and communication

Institutions

Publishing: Readerships, sites ofuse, distribution, circulation Collections: Public and private repositories, libraries, archives Educational institutions: Sites of formalized study

Transportation and communication Stucture and inatrucu distribution and dissemination

Economic systems Patterns trke

Legal and political issues Intellectual poperty, sohp, licensing

Entertainment and media industry Forms of consumption in changing venues, formats, and audiences

Events and trends Political, cultural, and environmental developments

Cultural conditions and attitudes Social institutions and cultural attitudes situate designed artifacts within specific networks of use and circulation. In turn, these institutions themselves are constantly being modified by cultural changes in technology, work settings and labor configurations, economic viability, and political factors. Printing and publishing contributed to the spread of literacy and creation of communities of knowledge. As means of reproducing and disseminating knowledge evolve, the notion of the public sphere as a realm of shared information and common assumptions changes. Patterns of commerce and trade—such as the way we have come to recognize branding as a feature of our cultural landscape—have economic and social implications that far outstrip formal properties of the objects in question. For example, the graphic language of brand design is not just about creating a concise, recognizable visual identity but about generating image-value within competitive markets. The economics of graphic design are also bound up with political battles over the shape and ownership of discourse, intellectual property, and censorship mechanisms. Every graphic artifact mediates social relations. Design shapes communication, and communication systems exert an enormous force in constructing the world we believe in. Showing how they do so is a challenging task.

## Specific concrete examples

## Public discourse

Civic or commercial notices. posters, flyers, signage, billboards. int-of-purchase displays, pop-up ads

## Mass-circulation publications

Magazines, newspapers, journals, and other print media supported by advertising

## Graphic commodities

Books and publications for entertainment, instruction, or reference, such as comics, novels, encyclopedias, bibles, cookbooks. and manuals

## Information graphics

Menus, maps, timetables, charts, graphs, and other displays of quantitative or qualitative data

## Useful ephemera

Tickets. money, stamps, receipts, and other graphic items designed transact or record an exchange or to perform another function

## Identity and branding systems

Logos, letterhead, packaging, and signage for corporate and public entities

## Milestones

Individuals. works, or campaigns of note

Graphic artifacts Discussion of any particular bit of imagery leads immediately into rich cultural inquiry. Whether images present bodies or objects, worlds for study or fantasy consumption, they can be readily taken apart—although the longer history of their forms may be obscure. Less evident is the way in which the structure of information as a system and a rhetoric establishes an argument and persuades a reader of the authority of its presentation. Underlying such systems are aesthetic principles, often founded on belief systems and attitudes toward form that have their roots in classicism, romanticism, and rationalism. Even fundamental values like clarity and legibility, or harmony and proportion, are fraught with cultural baggage.

Critical principles When we realize that graphic design shapes our conceptual horizons as well as our visible world, we have to come to terms with the way our view of what is possible-or not possible—within human experience, action, and understanding is embodied in communication media. If all artifacts serve a purpose, as we have said, then so do histories. A critical history tries to unmask the ideological bases that are concealed by a conventional history. By definition, any seamless linear story is false, concealing the multiple layers of disjunction and tension at work in a culture. The critical approach offered here brings particular themes and issues to the fore. Cutting across historical periods with thematic categories, this approach implies that many of the issues that come into focus in any one historical moment might be useful in examining another. To promote this investigation, we have compiled a basic list of critical principles.

## No graphic object is discrete or isolated.

## All cultural expressions participate in systems of production.

the object as an expression of social relations.)

## All designed communications serve vested interests.

## In most cases, these interests are concealed

## by the apparent message of the work.

(Who paid for it? What do they hope to gain? Follow the money.)

## Ease of consumption is expensive to produce.

(An inverse relation exists between production values and effortlessness of consumption, such that many social, environmental, or human costs are hidden in the seamless look of a final product.)

## The more "natural" something appears,

## the more culturally indicative it is.

oot shows he "ay thise"—they nstu words-e and then pass it ff s "atal.)

## Anything that claims to be universal is highly suspicious.

l . Why is this bias being imposed? Who benefits? Whose values are excluded?)

## Every graphic artifact constitutes an exchange

## among individuals, groups, or entities.

(Graphic objects are part of a network of communication.)

## Meaning is made not transmitted.

(Every act of reading and viewing is a creative act of interpretation.)

## Communication is a dynamic system.

(A work creates a viewer as much as a viewer creates a work.)

## Technology is not determinant.

(Cultural receptivity shapes the use of any new medium or invention. Something wil otcatch n without a collective dee or its development

Graphic Design History

A Critical Guide

![](images/b7813447e2b50e17704e177b22eebba1c042ee76dba0ffb75120ebc3c118f245.jpg)

# Prehistoric Prelude to Graphic Design 5,002700

Basic graphic concepts such as figure/ground distinctions, scale relations, ognization f images, and efectsof color wee developed in prehistic sign and image making.

The processes of early image production imply forethought and preparation of pigments, surfaces, and tools—that distinguish conscious design from incidental traces.

Graphic communication has always depended upon the design of symbols and images that are recognized according to the conventions of a community.

The design of prehistoric symbolic forms suggests that early humans did not simply record their needs and activities but placed great value on the representation of ideas.

0.1 Stenciled hand, Pech Merle, France, 23,000-18,000 BcE. The urge to make marks is a fundamental human impulse. Creating external symbols gives back an image of the self while leaving a sign in the world. The stenciled hand shown here is thousands of years old, but similar signs can be found throughout history. Graphic design arises from the relation between that basic impulse and the system of communication that connects individuals within a culture. The impulse toward personal expression and compliance with convention are the twin engines of all graphic design.

Connecting contemporary graphic design to visual art in the earliest periods of human civilization might seem like a stretch. But, in fact, Stone Ageartists establishedthe basic conventions essential to graphi design as early as 35,000 years ago. These foundations are now so familiar that they are taken for granted. Precisely because they are so fundamental to later visual communication, the remarkable achievements of prehistoric artists deserve attention. Prehistoric artists organized surfaces to support fgure and ground distinctions, establishing the basis on which signs and images could be produced and read. They prepared specialized materials for works of art, demonstrating that graphic expression was a deliberate process, not simply the result of impulsive acts. Finally, they shared a common visual vocabulary, indicating that they worked within a system of socially recognized conventions. Each of these characteristics is a uniquely human phenomenon. We are the only species that has a developed sign system abstracted from literal meaning and immediate circumstances. Human signs have a dual identity as representations and communications. As representations, human signs stand in for or refer to things that are absent, such as ideas, objects, feelings, or events. As communications, they enable social exchanges among people and across time and space.

We can point to prehistoric art as the origin of graphic design because it presents clear evidence of conscious decisions about form. Stone Age sculptures and cave paintings exhibit deliberate intention and a high level of craft. They are designed objects that often feature decorative images and marks. Their striking shapes, styles, and imagery carry information about other social and ritual practices and thus have been the focus of anthropological study. The material record of this early period has much to tell us about the technological and cultural conditions of image and object production. Deliberate mark-making, the preparation of materials and surfaces, the use of conventional signs, the cultural primacy of

![](images/fdda2e58676f0536b52eeeaccedb0e7c0113c892f9e8f1e7283efe78fd4c38d4.jpg)

0.2 Map of migrations. The theory of a common African origin for all human beings has been validated repeatedly by the scientific methods of modern anthropology. The Olduvai Gorge in present-day Tanzania was once a lush lake area and is considered the geographical starting point of all subsequent dispersal. Biologically modern humans emerged by 200,000 BcE and began to migrate into Ethiopia and other parts of Africa. By 100,000 BcE, migrations had reached the ancient Middle East and spread through the Mediterranean region. From there they continued into all the land masses of the so-called Old and New Worlds, reaching North America as early as 50,000 Bce. Archaeological digs have uncovered settlements that attest to the adaptability of the human species to widely varied habitats, although we ali live within the so-called Goidilocks zone—a temperate climate that supports human life and the air, water, animals, and vegetables required to sustain it.

communication, and the expression of ideas in form, are the legacy of prehistoric graphic work to the present-day designer (Fig. 0.1).

Evolutionary foundations of communication The history of graphic design begins with the emergence of a distinct human culture. In Africa, between three and four million years ago, the earliest hominids (proto-humans) began to walk upright, distinguishing themselves from other apes. This posture is considered the precondition for the evolution of modern humans. Standing upright freed our hands to make tools. Anthropologists suggest that this physical change also produced new kinds of behaviors, including increased socialization and use of language. Hands play a primary role in the creation of symbolic forms. The ability to make marks and signs depends upon motor skills and conceptual thought. Eye, hand, and mind are all coordinated in the human designer whose capacity to devise symbolic systems is the direct result of these earlier physical and social developments (Fig. 0.2).

Language and design Archaeological evidence suggests that spoken language preceded writing. As early as 150,000200,000 years ago, human beings apparently used language to organize complex social activities such as hunts. Although spoken language leaves no trace, the design of prehistoric tools reveals capacities for abstract thought that are

0.3 Neolithic carved tool, Mas-d'Azil, France, 16,000-9000 BcE. Carved tools and other decorated artifacts have been found among human settiements dating back to the Upper Paleolithic period (the last phase of the Old Stone Age), about 40,000-10,000 BCE, although humans first made crude tools as early as 2.6 million years ago. Design clearly mattered in the crafting of these objects. Considerable effort went into shaping and decorating them with figures. The care taken suggests that the object's symbolic purpose was as important as its ability to cut or carve effectively. The ability to create meaningful patterns and attach symbolic value to forms is a distinctly human characteristic. Other animals may produce exquisitely shaped or highly organized nests, dances, or songs, but no other animal makes recognizable effigies or adds decoration to its tools or environment

Loic Hamon/Art Resource/Reunion des Musees Nationaux

![](images/2aa402a55ad4ab95a7d3155b20972badb4b6e0539022140b623e0f39cdb1f8e2.jpg)

0.4 Venus of Willendorf, Austria, 22,000-20,000 BCE. Carved animals and human forms were common in the Upper Paleolithic period. The Venus of Willendorf is a small figure that was readily held in the hand. Her swollen breasts, full belly, and wide hips suggest fertility. Traces of pigment remain on the figure, but the form's tactile quality has been interpreted as being of greater significance than its appearance. Such a figure may have been used as a magic charm, held during lovemaking to ensure procreation, but any such interpretation is highly speculative. What is evident is that the work of shaping and finishing the figure has made it seem like a natural expression in stone—we see "her" first and the stone after. The symbolic force of the design has overwhelmed its materiality and the process by which it was made.

![](images/12f972538dc811de96b4e5d070c89a1086850a02e3f22ce1a52aae1db742fec4.jpg)

essential to language. Such objects provide very early evidence of design as a mode of intentional form-giving. In making these tools, human designers understood the idea of shapes for use—independent of their embodiment by any particular object. Early humans could generalize the design of a form to suit a purpose and repeat it across the production of many individual objects. They had a model of the design in mind to guide tool making (Fig. 0.3).

The general category of a tool, such as "something to poke the earth with," can be fulfilled by any number of objects. This capacity to distinguish an abstract idea, or model, from a specific realization is a basic component of both design and language. Many creatures use signs to exchange information, but only humans have the capacity to represent absent and abstract phenomena in symbolic form. Animal sign systems are based on direct references to things that are present and physical. Their methods of communicating are often closely related to the messages they carry, as in chemical trails (ants), flight patterns (bees), markings (territorial animals), display (mating rituals), and cries and sounds (mating calls, danger signals). Animals have no capacity for abstraction and no developed sense of history or time, at least according to current scientific study. By contrast, human language and graphic systems use signs that are divorced from physical evidence or concrete references. Capable of representing things, ideas, actions, and abstractions, the signs of human language are socially produced. With these conventionalized visual and verbal gestures and forms, any information, emotion, or idea can be translated.

Early graphic forms The earliest works of visual communication were produced in three separate periods before the invention of writing (in about 3000 BcE). Art from the earliest of these, the Stone Age, or Paleolithic period, dates from about 35,000 BcE. Although visual design, art, and tool making are closely linked at this stage, all the founding principles of graphic communication can be distinguished in the formal expression and conventional signs of the carved figures, decorated tools, and cave paintings that remain from this period (Fig. 0.4).

Stone Age artists in Western Europe were part of Cro-Magnon culture. Collective hunting was one of the first organized activities and was recorded in images. Cro-Magnon artists approached their subjects realistically. Dating from 15,000-10,000 BCE, renderings of bison, deer, and other game in the cave paintings of Altamira, Spain, and Lascaux, France, are still

0.5 Cave painting, Altamira, Spain, 16,000-9000 BcE. When the cave paintings at Altamira, Spain, were discovered in the late 1870s, they created a sensation. Far from being primitive expressions by unskilled artists, these paintings were sophisticated at every level of conception and execution. Because they were recognized as finely made, they challenged concepts of progress in art. The figures are clear and distinct, and the ground on which they appear is a wellprepared and organized surface. These paintings are not only powerful aesthetic expressions, they also document a long history. Successive waves of climate change, for example, were recorded in cave art. Images of reindeer, painted on earlier layers, were replaced by those of ibexes and other animals as the ice sheets receded in later millennia. Although drawn from memory, the images are naturalistic. What is remarkable, however, is how much similarity exists among cave paintings. This striking fact shows that a distinct set of visual conventions had been established for producing artworks. Although it would be a stretch to call the world of prehistoric cave painters a design community, rules governing what was formally possible and acceptable were followed by these artists. The artists did not simply invent visual solutions at random in response to circumstances or impulses. They produced highly organized works of art, designed according to formal and cultural conventions. This social basis for form arguably marks the beginning of graphic communication.

![](images/1bc1264a8a5d34d4a5d200dce32e478955ce3edc2e25e59ec13a587989e04818.jpg)

recognizable. But the graphic qualities of these images go beyond likeness. The composition, elegance of line, care in production, and considerations of scale, color, and pattern in these paintings display a considerable investment in the quality of visual design. These artists did not simply use raw materials lifted from the campfire but mixed their pigments deliberately. They worked in sites that were difficult to access and often layered their images with successive drawings. These facts suggest that such works played an active part in performances and were not simply meant to remain as static records (Fig. 0.5).

Communicating ideas and beliefs The traditional interpretation of cave art is that it was a form of sympathetic hunting magic. More recent theories propose that these paintings had an intellectual purpose. According to this interpretation, these images followed from the notion that something that was "good to eat" might also be pleasurable to think about. The paintings not only responded to needs (or fulfilled a superstitious function) but also corresponded to ideas. These graphic forms are now considered to be the symbolic expression of a worldview rather than simply an attempt to gain power over the uncertain forces of nature. The realization that prehistoric graphic signs embody values and express beliefs places them on a continuum that includes contemporary visual design.

We have very little sense of the identity of Stone Age artists. The cave painter might have been a shaman possessed of spiritual power, a loner sent off to a far corner of the cave, an idle doodler taking pleasure in aesthetic expression, a craftsperson especially capable of fine work, or some combination of these. In any case, evidence of advanced material preparation implies a pause between conception and execution that allows for critical reflection upon the act of making. The gap between thinking and making is crucial to all forms of design.

![](images/b49b795cceb7c6669a9a1967a4c003fedac874285fc5cce8cc37b35ff4589b46.jpg)

0.6 Clay seal, Mesopotamia, 3000 BCE. Clay seals dating back as early as the fourth millennium Bce have been found in archaeological sites of the ancient city of Uruk, present-day Iraq. These seals provide evidence of a flourishing agricultural and urban economy. In the act of creating individual patterns that served as signs of ownership, ancient scribes established basic design conditions and conventions. For instance, a pattern had to be distinctive if it was to function as a signature. A surface or material support had to be prepared and unified so that it defined the space of a message. Signs and marks had to be consistent—or intentionally varied—in scale, proximity, orientation, and groupings. Pigments and colors, where used, had to be refined and deliberately applied. In other words, basic elements of composition and display were developed in the graphic codes of these early impressions made by seals rolled across soft clay.

The invention of proto-writing The Mesolithic period (or Middle Stone Age)—about 10,000-5000 BCE—is characterized byg the development of agriculture, leading to the rise of cities and civic organization in the ancient Middle East. It is also marked by a huge leap in graphic communication: the appearance of proto-writing. This development is linked to increased population and changes in the social organization of human settlements. City structures took social and cultural form within clearly defined boundaries (urban design in the ancient city of Jericho can be traced to its earliest walls, which were 13 feet high by 8000 BCE). The invention of proto-writing was a direct consequence of successful cultivation of hard grains (which were capable of being stored without rotting) at the beginning of the Neolithic period (or New Stone Age)—about 5000-2000 BCE—in the ancient Mesopotamian region. When more of this grain could be grown in a season than was needed for immediate or local use, accounting systems became necessary to help monitor ownership, distribution, and storage. These systems relied, for the first time, on symbols designed to signify numerical value and specific objects in the world. After about 8000 BCE, the numeric and pictographic signs of this proto-writing were used on tokens and clay tablets to mark ownership and quantities of goods. These cultural changes took place much later among settlements in Northern Europe, Africa, and Asia—and even later in the Americas (Fig. 0.6).

Conclusion Graphic design depends on symbolic systems that are visually stable and shared by convention. The early evolution of human language and imagery laid crucial foundations for later developments in the design of all material objects, including graphic communications. Even in cave art, technologies of production—the care given to preparation of materials and tools—attest to an awareness of the creative process. The idea that graphic forms not only give shape to needs and desires but also express a view of the world is critical to all phases of human history. Distinctions of figure and ground that are fundamental to the organization of visual perception were made in cave art, and other aesthetic properties of composition, color, and imagery were developed. Later distinctions among different types of signs and symbol systems allowed the representation of numbers, words, and things to exist side by side without confusion. Graphic conventions for arranging and reading marks and signs on a surface were already in place at the end of the prehistoric era. These elements remain essential to visual communication and provide a basis that connects current design practices to this prehistoric prelude.

Timelines These lists situate graphic events in relation to other cultural and political markers. Like all timelines, they are selective, and bther juxtapositions inevitably suggestivfinterpretations.Clerly partial, timelines call attention to the framing effects of editing and to the artifice of any history. In early chapters, many dates are approximate.

Tools of the Trade These lists contain items that might be used by a graphic artist or designer for original production in the period corresponding to the chapter dates. They also contain technologies that would be used for reproduction in the period, even if not directly by a designer. (Any graphic artist or designer would need to be aware of available methods of reproduction in order to fashion images or text suitable to those methods.) Media, tools, material supports, and technologies of production and reproduction are thus included. These lists are by no means exhaustive but are meant to be suggestive. The timeframe within which an item appears does not necessarily correspond to the moment of its invention. And carryover from one period to another is, for the most part, implicit.

## Early Writing: Mark-making, Notation Systems, and Scripts 3000-500 BCE

The design of proto-writing systems introduced the first stable codes for graphic representation of language.

• ale the basis of any graphic communication system.

• Theelement visual  ysems werdfie  ndamental a varibles:hape izerientation, and postion.

The meaningful organization of signs depended on compositional principles of juxtaposition, sequence, hierarchy, and direction.

• we  u qic to function as a means of encoding ideas as well.

visual code agreed upon as a convention by a social group. The effects of writing as a form of social control and power spring from this consensus.

1.1 Marks as signatures, North America, seventeenth century. Signatures in the shape of totem animals attest to the authority of this document. The picture signs are not writing in the proper sense. They stand directly for a person not for language. These marks are "like" writing, but they are not part of a code that can be used to represent or express other statements. The presence of these pictorial signs alongside the linear script of the English document embodies the dialogue of literate and oral cultures. This missive was sent by the kings and sachems of the ancient Nations of the Susquehanna and Savannah to the kings and sachems of England in recognition of the good deeds of an agent of William Penn during the period of colonial settlement in Philadelphia.

Graphic media entered a new age with the coming of writing. By 3000 BCE, new tools for carving, inscribing, and marking were being developed and used on material supports of varying durability, such as clay, stone, papyrus, skin, bone, wax, metal, and wood. But the invention of writing required more than tools. Writing depended on the design of a stable visual code for the representation of language. This connection to language makes writing distinct from other visual signs that stand for ideas or things but not for speech or a linguistic system. Writing extended the principles of graphic organization that had been developed for image and symbol making. The shapes of the first glyphs, letters, and signs connect the earliest human marks to present-day alphabetic and character-based scripts through a continuous legacy. We still use versions of some of the earliest written symbols ever invented. From the outset, these symbols had the effect of changing the power of language by aligning it with the administration of culture. Writing orchestrated economic, political, religious, and other social activities. It expressed laws and social constraints that could be enforced by symbolic power rather than brute force. Whereas language distinguishes humans from other animals, writing introduces significant differences between oral and literate cultures. Oral communication is ephemeral and takes place in real-time circumstances that rely on presence. Writing communicates in the absence of its author. Enduring inscriptions transmit textual records to remote contexts (Fig. 1.1).

Mark-making Mark-making is the most basic form of graphic expression and design. Drawing a line in the sand or making a handprint on a wall is a direct sign of individual identity. Such imprints have a curious existence: a mark that is a sign of the self is also always other than the self.

1.2 Rongo Rongo script, Easter Island, date unknown. The Rongo Rongo script of Easter Island has an intriguingly exuberant rhythmic quality, but it has never been deciphered. Anthropologists now consider that it is probably not a written language but a set of signs used for ritual purposes. The script is not ancient, and scholars have suggested that it was invented by indigenous people exposed to European writing in the eighteenth century. Independent inventions of writing have occurred in many cultures. The graphic properties of this notation make it scriptlike because it contains marks and intervals that are clearly differentiated and nonpictorial elements that are neither physical traces of a hand or tool nor repeated motifs in a decorative pattern.

![](images/2e97fe3253af4ef593bd9dd30729b9672740ff1ffb8b827d00cff3a93c9bbf5f.jpg)

![](images/53c979559f598370fa24198325dd65abdb312aa97c88444bbcb88bc14bc227d1.jpg)

Once made, marks exist independently of their makers. A line makes a division, whether in physical space as a boundary or in an intellectual zone to define a category. Differentiating between marked and unmarked things is a conceptual act of enormous significance. We might even suggest that the idea of difference forms the basis of human knowledge, and that the making of a mark is the primary way of inscribing such difference. On this simple principle of differentiation we can structure the complicated oppositions of I/Thou, me/you, this/that, self/other, subject/object, which form our understanding of the world. Marks are not only different from each other but also distinct from the ground on which they are made.

## True writing is a graphic expression of language. This relation

1.3 Painted stones, Mas-d'Azil, France, 10,000 sce. These are some of the earliest remains of systematic markmaking. The regularity of the repeated marks and their careful graphic execution indicate deliberate intention. Though too few in number to represent a language, they may have been conventionalized representations of things. The stick-figure form is referred to as the "hailelujah man" because it appears to be a person with arms raised in jubilation. This sign, like the other simple forms on these rocks, is common to many so-called primitive systems of marks. Even when they develop independently, human scripts often share formal characteristics. This does not suggest that a universal code underlies all writing but rather, pragmatically, that simple lines can be combined in a certain number of fairly predictable ways.

Perhaps even more profoundly, in any graphic code, marks are different from the intervals that separate them (Fig. 1.2).

Marks and signs alone do not constitute writing. The earliest marked objects that remain from prehistoric periods are the stones from the Mas-d'Azil area in France. They present enough regularity in their repeated use of crosses, waves, and sinewy patterns to suggest a conventional code. But the meaning of these marks relied on a context now lost, and the system to which they belonged seems insufficiently developed to represent a language. These stones may have been signs for objects rather than signs for language. Their designs do not appear to be numerous or varied enough to be a verbal code. This contrast is crucial. True writing is a graphic expression of language. This relation makes writing unique among visual forms of expression and fundamentally different from drawing or painting. But systems on the verge of becoming writing reveal the basic conditions for all letter and notation design: a deliberately identified surface, distinctions between figure and ground, differentiation of mark and interval, and principles of visual organization (sequence, scale, orientation, and juxtaposition) (Fig. 1.3).

1.4 Clay tokens, Mesopotamia, fourth millennium Bce. These regularly shaped objects served as a form of notation for business transactions. Their shapes each corresponded to a different category of agricultural product. The marks on their surfaces were highly schematic and used for detailed accounting. When these tokens were pressed into wet clay, their imprints were part of a graphic system in which relative size, shape, orientation, and groupings were all significant. These tokens demonstrate the emergence of a set of sophisticated graphic principles. The spatial organization of the surface and the elaboration of rules governing the use of visual signs are evidence of graphic refinement. This notation system is still not considered writing because "writing" is reserved for the representation of language, and these tokens are basically cash register receipts.

Art Resource/Reunion des Musees Nationaux

![](images/09ef5989d2f8357fd0c54abdaa9b04518682760891f2152f772c4c963838fcd9.jpg)

![](images/1f883851302755f37a51b1d0fd0df16c2053e7f5b0fa72e7583b452b30c17453.jpg)

Notation Numerical notation systems of the ancient Near East are considered proto-writing. Unlike pre-writing systems, these forms of notation had stable meanings and values. Archaeologist Denise Schmandt-Besserat discovered the earliest evidence of a token system that dates to about 8000-6000 BcE. Since her initial find, researchers have unearthed hundreds of distinctly shaped clay tokens. Round, triangular, cylindrical, ovoid, and otherwise fashioned, these small clay forms were used for recording business transactions in a thriving agricultural society. Differentiated by shape and inscribed with distinct markings, these tokens were made to correspond to approximately sixteen categories that were essential to that economy: oil, cattle, grain, and so on. The marks on these

## makes writing unique among visual forms of expression.

1.5 Indus Valley script, Mohenjo-Daro, 2600-1800 BcE. The remains of this proto-Indic script from the Indus Valley are few in number, and most contain a very limited set of signs. But the arrangement and combination of apparently figurative and numerical elements on this artifact bear a striking resemblance to cuneiform tokens found in Uruk and may have had a common origin. The Indus Valley script seems to have been used only for a few centuries before disappearing The complicated graphic forms on this tablet would be arduous to use in a system requiring frequent repetition.

© Scala/Art Resource

clay tokens were often schematic, challenging the notion that the origins of writing were entirely pictorial. The impressions made by the tokens were precursors to the wedge-shaped signs of cuneiform, the oldest system of actual writing (Fig. 1.4).

During the several thousand years in which they were used, the signs on these clay tokens made a critical conceptual leap. At first, they were meant to represent specific things in schematic images. Then they came to function as abstract representations of categories of things or ideas. For instance, from the practice of continually representing four oxen, four sacks of grain, or four containers of oil came the idea that four could be represented by a sign. Thus, the concept of quantity was abstracted from direct, concrete counting. To the contemporary mind, the enormity of such a leap is almost impossible to appreciate. Yet to realize that many categories of thought are similarly abstract provides an insight into the contribution of notation and writing to human culture. The design problem implied in this shift is equally profound. What should a sign look like when it represents an abstract idea ("four-ness") rather than a thing? Designers of symbolic signage systems struggle with the same issues today (Fig. 1.5).

![](images/36b0785c40e0fe5cab30fe3f1847b6c0fee64d4a471501d1945a57db70d1c7ae.jpg)

1.6a Oracle bones, China, 1300 BcE. The earliest forms of writing in China appeared on bones used for divination, dating from 1300 Bce, more than a thousand years later than the development of writing in Egypt and the ancient Near East. Chinese oracle bones, such as this tortoise carapace, were cracked in the heat of a fire and rubbed with a darkening agent so that glyphs emerged in their surfaces. The signs became standardized as part of the Chinese character script over the next several centuries.

![](images/ea35546e6a4f96bcd98e76546aba67a9a7f28505a85ab25629414b1a6d1b994e.jpg)

## What is the written equivalent of an ironic inflection?

1.6b Chinese writing, archaic to modern. The transformation of Chinese characters from their pictorial origins to schematic signs happened with relative rapidity. Chinese characters most frequently represent either words or sounds, or some combination of the two. No writing system that required a sign for every word would be practical. The adaptation of characters for Japanese, Korean, and other languages demonstrates the versatility of Chinese script.

The word notation refers to signs that stand for categories and ideas in a stable graphic system but fall short of the ability to represent language or speech. The term writing, as we have said, is reserved for graphic forms that specifically express language. The relation of writing to spoken language is not simple-even in a system such as the alphabet in which letter signs are supposed to represent sounds. Many things said in speech cannot be represented in writing, and vice versa. Written and spoken forms are both expressions of language. But they are not necessarily identical to each other. What is the written equivalent of an ironic inflection? A whisper? A seductive tone of voice? Writing has many properties that derive from its graphic nature. What is the spoken equivalent of a hyphen? Or a decorative initial? Or a sans serif face? Or the division of text into two columns on a page?

Varieties of early writing Writing proper evolved in ancient Mesopotamia around 3200 BCE, in the Fertile Crescent that lies between the Tigris and Euphrates rivers. Egyptian writing may have been an independent development, or it may have evolved, in part, as an imitation

![](images/46cb4ff47b9dc70aba4b946df44e402a9a1c5fe8e5e21d6aae25131830377e46.jpg)

# What is the spoken equivalent of a hyphen or a sans serif face?

1.7 Clay tablet with cuneiform, Iraq, 3000 BE. The signs on this clay tablet are considered an early form of cuneiform writing. Made with many small strokes, the signs seem to be drawings of particular things. The pictorial origins of cuneiform can be traced in these and other artifacts. But these signs are considered writing because they can be related to the sounds in the Sumerian language. The visual structure of cuneiform tablets is well ordered. The surface is usually divided into columns and/or rows, either by visible or implied lines. The size and spacing of the marks are regular, and their rhythmic calligraphy has a momentum that carries the eye forward. The marks are grouped according to a system which is as elaborate visually as a code for language is verbally. The unwritten rules of this system are fundamental to visual communication, and, although scripts and letterforms have changed in the five thousand years since these first written records were produced, the graphic principles of size, sequence, grouping, and ordering still apply.

of Mesopotamian cuneiform scripts. Writing in the Indus Valley (presentday Pakistan) may also have been borrowed or adapted from cuneiform. In China, writing emerged later and apparently independently, although some debate exists about cultural influences from Europe to Asia. Glyphic writing originated in Mesoamerican cultures in advance of contact with Europeans and has its own graphic rules (Figs. 1.6a-c).

Writing and sign making are directly related to administrative tasks and the social structures needed to enable civil societies. For instance, by 3000 BCE, the state bureaucracy of Uruk in Mesopotamia had ten numerical systems in use, each with a specialized sphere of activity. The management of tasks associated with irrigation systems led to the invention of maps in Sumer by the end of the third millennium BCE. Writing codified law, and its enforcement came to supplement (and sometimes replace) violence as an instrument of power (Fig. 1.7).

Cuneiform was first used to represent ancient Sumerian, one of a handful of ancient languages that have never been deciphered. The number of cuneiform signs suggests that they represented words in a logographic script. Signs recovered from sites like the city of Uruk in ancient Sumeria

## Cuneiform spread the idea of writing in the ancient Near East.

1.8 Development of cuneiform. The need for efficiency in writing systems propelled simplification from pictorial to schematic signs. A stylus in wet clay made some marks more easily than others, and the orientation as well as the shape and combination of these marks changed as writing became stabilized. Sumerian linear and cuneiform marks from about 2400 BCE were adopted by successive peoples in the same geographic regions: Akkadians, Babylonians, and Assyrians each used cuneiform for their own distinct language. Not only was cuneiform graphically efficient, but also it represented an enormous conceptual advancement: it was a fully developed, adaptable written code for language.

indicate pictographic origins, but some debate exists about whether these signs derived from the more schematic imagery of token notation. In any case, these Sumerian pictograms themselves soon became schematized so that they could be drawn in a few quick strokes of the stylus on a soft tablet. The cuneiform script was adopted by the Akkadians, a Semiticspeaking people who invaded the Mesopotamian region about 2400 BCE. The adoption of a writing system developed for one language by another is a pattern that is repeated many times in the history of scripts. The Akkadians distilled a set of cuneiform signs that represented syllables. These voweland-consonant combinations were numerous but functioned more efficiently than signs for every word (Fig. 1.8).

In a subsequent wave of migration into the same region around 1600 BCE, Hittites also adopted cuneiform, this time to represent their Indo-European language. The cuneiform script spread throughout the ancient Near East and was used extensively across various cultures and languages for diplomatic purposes in the third and second millennia BCE. Cuneiform reached its apex of popularity in about 1400 BCE when its diplomatic use extended throughout a region that included present-day Iraq, Turkey, Syria,

![](images/7851d624188ba2dd4d66f60c8bbb48d8f997101f0d6fa771c4a32eae3e93131c.jpg)

## 1.9 Origins and development of

alphabetic scripts. The incredible variety of forms into which alphabets have evolved creates the illusion of a proliferation of different scripts. In fact, the alphabets of the world are derived from a single source whose sequence and basic forms provided a foundation for scripts in Africa, the Near East, Asia, Europe, and the Americas. Chinese characters are the other successful script form and, like the alphabet, were adopted and modified for use across a remarkable number of languages.

Egypt, Palestine, Lebanon, and Israel. It was used by language groups from Indo-European, Afro-Asiatic, and other families. The latest recorded use of cuneiform (about 50 cE) occurred in the study of astronomy, a science that had Babylonian roots (Fig. 1.9).

From the example of cuneiform another important principle emerges: scripts and languages do not have a one-to-one relation to each other. Just as religious identities, language groups, and national boundaries do not map directly onto each other, so scripts are adopted by various peoples who modify them for their own use, often changing their signs in the process. Variation in the shape of scripts may result from available production

![](images/b27b59b5315188f7f2cac3f9f4cfb1ade55576199e643e24c5b4e163704c9768.jpg)

![](images/ba509261e61e17b02fe23f8e7a0e4cba00e9255e9efa50587377855d741895fe.jpg)

## Scripts and languages do not have a one-to-one relationship.

1.10 Language families. Scripts and languages developed independently. Although more than two dozen language families are known throughout the globe, only two major scripts are used: alphabetic writing and scripts derived from Chinese characters. The flexibility and efficiency of the alphabet have made it particularly adaptable for use in representing the basic sounds of many, often unrelated, spoken languages. The archaeological evidence for the development of language in humans is linked to the evolution of the shape of the skull and organs of speech. The developed palate, tongue, and mouth organs found in Homo sapiens remains suggest that language could have emerged by about 60,000 scE. The question of the relation of world languages to each other is much debated. Although a common origin may be possible, the spontaneous appearance of language as a specieswide adaptation is also credible. Speculation about the historical relation of languages is based on the structure of sounds, morphemes, syntax, and other features of grammar.

technologies (clay, wax, stone, etc.). Inversely, the limited number of graphic elements from which scripts tend to be constructed can lead to formal resemblance among unrelated scripts. With the advent of stable writing systems, the record of human existence becomes linked to texts. These offer evidence of cultural developments alongside artifacts such as pottery, tools, jewelry, textiles, dwellings, burial sites, and other remains (Fig. 1.10).

The spread of writing as idea and script The idea of writing that developed with cuneiform may have been as influential as the actual script. Both spread to other areas in the ancient Near East. Although Egyptian hieroglyphics, the short-lived Indus Valley script, and various Cretan pictographic and syllabic systems are different in appearance from cuneiform, the inspiration for their development may have come from contact with the wedge-shaped system that had been widespread in the ancient Near and Middle East. A host of different scripts developed throughout this region in the second millennium BCE, varying in form from hybrids of cuneiform and pictographic scripts to highly developed schematic sign systems. But only a few sustained widespread use.

![](images/d2ad33b003bb9fbdb82c2885f4807cd1bc185fa1710a723c85a283d863be335e.jpg)

The oldest artifacts containing Egyptian hieroglyphics—from the Greek words hiero ("sacred") and glyph ("carving")—show them almost fully developed. Many of their standard shapes were already set by the time they were adopted in 3200-3100 BcE. Little evidence exists for dating Egyptian hieroglyphs earlier than this. Connections between the Egyptian writing system and those of the ancient Near East are still debated as evidence of influence along the trade routes that passed through Syria. From a graphic perspective, one of the fascinating features of hieroglyphic writing is that it is organized according to underlying grid structures rather than in a fixed word order. Hieroglyphics can be arranged in right-to-left, left-to-right, or up-and-down rows. They were usually composed to achieve maximum spatial efficiency inside invisible but implied quadrants on a stone or papyrus surface. This rationalization of a surface area, combined with attention to the direction and arrangement of signs within a picture plane, demonstrates considerable graphic sophistication. The flexibility of this system is shown by the variable orientation of hieroglyphic animal and human profiles. The Narmer Palette is considered one of the earliest artifacts of Egyptian writing. Like its earliest counterparts among the artifacts of Uruk, the Narmer Palette contains all three major forms of graphic communication: images, texts, and numerical signs (Fig. 1.11).

1.12a Hieroglyphics, lintel from the reign of Sesostris III, Egypt, 1887-1850 BCE. An underlying grid organizes the signs into quadrants according to a principle of graphic efficiency rather than grammar. Hieroglyphics were meaningful as representations of words and syllables, but their direction and groupings were also significant. Signs in proximity were read as a unit. Note that a graphic system constrains the size of these signs. Objects that are very unlike in scale are allotted the same unitary area in conformance with a distinct design principle. Although figurative in their appearance, hieroglyphics could represent words (logograms), sounds (phonograms), or categories (determinatives). Determinatives, or classifiers. were used to clarify the meaning of sign combinations. For instance, one combination of signs means "to be stable," unless it is accompanied by the image of a sparrow, the sign for evil, in which case it is read as "to be bad." Réunion des Musées Nationaux/Art Resource/Reunion des Musees Nationaux

1.12b Hieratic and Demotic scripts. The evolution of a hieratic script from hieroglyphic forms provided a far more efficient system that could be written quickly with a brush and ink. Demotic script represented an even more schematic, informal writing form used for daily occasions. The more elaborate pictorial forms of hieroglyphics were used for formal, official purposes. After the Greek conquest of Egypt around 332 BCE, by Alexander the Great, hieroglyphics took on their arcane and esoteric associations. The priestly caste introduced a deliberate obscurity into the use of hieroglyphics as a way of resisting Greek influence.

![](images/deb6ef8bdee53d9bbc28b37e256e0f26341fd4dd14016b9b960019eed3aa7a02.jpg)

![](images/eb5d2375edbf9dd593f71a107fe8c0c52b7748a8ffedfdc63f101423917192c0.jpg)

Hieroglyphics did not spread. Other pictographic scripts of the Mediterranean region did not copy their visual forms, even if some of these scripts borrowed the idea of writing from their Egyptian neighbors. Both hieroglyphics and cuneiform fell out of use, although for different reasons. Hieroglyphics were banned when Christianity became the state religion in Egypt under the rule of Theodosius in the fourth century CE, while cuneiform was displaced from popularity by the more efficient alphabetic system beginning in the second millennium BCE (Figs. 1.12a and 1.12b).
